question: Explain what a data structure is and provide two examples of linear data structures.
expectedPoints: A data structure is a way of organizing, managing, and storing data to enable efficient access and modification.
An array is a collection of items stored at contiguous memory locations, allowing for O(1) access.
A linked list is a linear collection of data elements (nodes) where each node points to the next, allowing for efficient insertions/deletions.

question: Compare and contrast a stack and a queue, explaining their fundamental principles and providing a real-world example for each.
expectedPoints: A stack is a Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) structure where the last element added is the first one removed.
A queue is a First-In, First-Out (FIFO) structure where the first element added is the first one removed.
A real-world example of a stack is the undo function in an application (reversing the most recent action).
A real-world example of a queue is a line of people waiting for a service (the first person in line is served first).
Both are abstract data types that can be implemented using arrays or linked lists.

question: Describe the process of inserting a new node into a binary search tree (BST). Explain the properties that must be maintained, walk through the algorithmic steps, and analyze the time complexity in the average and worst-case scenarios.
expectedPoints: A BST must maintain the property that for any given node, all values in its left subtree are less than its value, and all values in its right subtree are greater.
The insertion process begins at the root, comparing the new value to the current node to determine if it should go left or right, recursively traversing until an empty child pointer is found.
The new node is then created and attached as a leaf at this empty position.
The average-case time complexity for insertion is O(log n), assuming the tree is relatively balanced.
The worst-case time complexity is O(n), which occurs if the tree becomes a degenerate linked list (e.g., when inserting sorted data).
